conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by asking:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm asking about my child Alex. They're 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me start by asking a few questions about your situation. First:

**Are you responsible for Alex? That is, do you live with Alex and are you responsible for their day-to-day care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me actually. They live with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand the situation a bit better.

**Do you contribute towards Alex's upbringing in any way, such as paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay (currently at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit rate) towards Alex's care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially to Alex's upbringing.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Let me ask one more important question to complete the assessment:

**Does Alex live with their other parent who is responsible for their day-to-day care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex lives with their other parent who looks after them day-to-day.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me read the official guidance to ensure I give you an accurate assessment of your eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. Based on the information you've provided and the official UK government guidance, I can now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on your circumstances, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

**Here's why:**

To qualify for Child Benefit, you need to be responsible for the child. According to UK government rules, you're usually considered responsible if:
- You live with the child, **OR**
- You're paying at least the same amount as the Child Benefit rate towards their care (such as for food, clothes, or pocket money)

Since Alex doesn't live with you and you're not currently making financial contributions to their upbringing, you don't meet the responsibility criteria.

**Important to note:** Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. In this case, Alex's other parent who provides their day-to-day care would be the eligible person to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex comes to live with you or if you start making regular financial contributions towards their upkeep - your eligibility could change.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: According to UK Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child. You're usually considered responsible if either: (1) you live with them, or (2) you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (e.g., on food, clothes, or pocket money). In this case, Alex does not live with you, and you confirmed you are not currently contributing financially to Alex's upbringing. Therefore, you do not meet the responsibility criteria. Since Alex lives with their other parent who provides day-to-day care, that parent would be the one eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and based on the circumstances, you would not qualify.

Alex does not live with the claimant, and the claimant does not contribute financially to Alex's upbringing. Alex lives with their other parent who provides day-to-day care, making that parent the eligible claimant for Child Benefit.
